Authors: Biswas, G.; Debelak, Kenneth A.; Kawamura, K.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Debelak, Kenneth A. (6603833210); Kawamura, Kazuhiko (57202749216)
Title: Applications of qualitative modeling to knowledge-based risk assessment studies
Source title: Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Industrial and Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence and Expert Systems, IEA/AIE 1989
Abstract: Risk assessment of technological processes (chemical and power plants, electro-mechanical systems) is a complex process that requires enumeration of all possible failure modes, their probability of occurrence, and their consequences. Traditionally such studies have been performed by a committee of expert engineers with diverse backgrounds. This paper discusses the use of qualitative modeling techniques based on deriving behavior from structural descriptions and causal reasoning to aid automating and enhancing the risk analysis process. Hierarchical schemes are used for describing component structure, and system functionality is derived from a set of primitive functions and parameters defined for the domain. The system uses these models to automatically generate fault and event networks for hypothesized fault situations specified by users. © 1989 ACM.

Authors: Cannon, R.; Moore, Philip; Tansathein, Dusit; Strobel, J.; Kendall, C.G.S.C.; Biswas, G.; Bezdek, J.
Author full names: Cannon, Robert L. (35581592300); Moore, Philip D. (7403684806); Tansathein, Dusit (6504288837); Strobel, John (35860656700); Kendall, Christopher George St Clement (7102153543); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898)
Title: Expert system as a component of an integrated system for oil exploration
Source title: Conference Proceedings - IEEE SOUTHEASTCON
Abstract: A description is given of the Expert Explorer System, a tool for analyzing areas with the potential for oil production. It has database, pattern recognition, simulation, report generation, and expert system components. The expert system is an essential element of the system because it assists the user in organizing and abstracting data in a manner such that the characteristics of an area under consideration may be compared with the characteristics of other known areas.

Authors: Smith, J.A.; Biswas, G.
Author full names: Smith, John A. (57124668000); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890)
Title: Multi-level models for diagnosis of complex electro-mechanical systems
Source title: Proceedings of SPIE - The International Society for Optical Engineering
Abstract: This paper discusses a knowledge-based system for diagnostic problem solving based on a multi-level representational structure and associated reasoning methods. © 1989 SPIE.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Yu, X.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Yu, Xudong (57196945820)
Title: Rule network for efficient implementation of a mixed-initiative reasoning scheme
Abstract: MIDST (Mixed Inferencing Dempster Shafer Tool) is a rule-based expert system shell that incorporates mixed-initiative reasoning and uncertain reasoning based on the Dempster-Shafer evidence combination scheme. This paper discusses the design and implementation of a rule network for MIDST that facilitates an efficient implementation of the mixed-initiative reasoning scheme.

Authors: Strobel, J.; Cannon, R.; Christopher, G.St.; Kendall, C.G.S.C.; Biswas, G.; Bezdek, J.
Author full names: Strobel, John (35860656700); Cannon, Robert L. (35581592300); Christopher, G. St (24377942600); Kendall, Christopher George St Clement (7102153543); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898)
Title: Interactive (SEDPAK) simulation of clastic and carbonate sediments in shelf to basin settings
Source title: Computers and Geosciences
Abstract: SEDPAK is an interactive computer simulation which erects models of sedimentary geometries by infilling a two-dimensional basin from both sides with a combination of clastic sediment or in situ and transported carbonate sediments. The simulation program is implemented in "C" on an Apollo DN3000 workstation using graphical plotting functions. Data entry, including the initial basin configuration, local tectonic behavior, sealevel curves, amount and source direction of clastic sediment, and the growth rates of carbonates as a function of water depth is performed interactively. The modeled geometries of clastic and carbonate sediments evolve through time and respond to depositional processes that include tectonic movement, eustasy, and sedimentation. Clastic modeling includes sedimentary bypass, erosion, and sedimentation in alluvial and coastal plains, marine shelf, basin slope, and basin floor settings. Carbonate modeling includes progradation, the development of hard grounds, downslope aprons, keep up, catch up, back step, and drowned reef facies as well as lagoonal and epeiric facies. Also included in the model are extensional vertical faulting of the basin, sediment compaction, and isostatic response to sediment loading. Sediment geometries are plotted on a graphics terminal as they are computed, so the user can immediately view the results. Then, based on these observations, parameters can be changed repeatedly and the program rerun until the user is satisfied with the resultant geometry. © 1989.
Author Keywords: Carbonate simulation; Clastic simulation; Eustatic sealevel; Sediment geometries

Authors: Biswas, G.; Oliff, M.; Sen, A.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Oliff, Michael D. (6602085201); Sen, Arun (8201466700)
Title: An expert decision support system for production control
Source title: Decision Support Systems
Abstract: The most crucial resource in manufacturing and production is human expertise. Unfortunately it is often the most costly to acquire. Corporate consulting fees and management training expenses testify to this end. Recent advances in artificial intelligence, decision support, and information processing offer a potential solution to this scarcity. The development of expert decision support systems in the production domain may prove to be the productivity theme of the decade. We present OASES (Operations AnalySis Expert System), designed using the XDSS framework proposed in [10] to emulate a consultant and aid management in troubleshooting manufacturing processes. © 1988.
Author Keywords: Decision Support Systems; Dempster Shafer Theory; Diagnostic Problem Solving; Inexact Reasoning; Knowledge Based Systems; Operations Analysis; Production Process

Authors: Neelakandan, H.; Biswas, G.; Matthews, M.
Author full names: Neelakandan, Harish (6507928639); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Matthews, Manton M. (7202525299)
Title: INTELLIGENT ASSISTANCE SYSTEM IN THE UNIX DOMAIN.
Abstract: The authors discuss the intelligent assistance component of USCSH, an assistance system that overcomes the shortcomings of a number of existing systems. USCSH has two components: (i) an active assistant that monitors the user's interaction with the system and, at appropriate times, suggests ways in which the user could use the system more effectively; and (ii) an intelligent assistant that responds to user queries about the software system posed in natural language. The authors emphasize the natural-language interface of the intelligent assistant. The interface is based on an ATN-based parser that converts the user's input to an internal frame-based format. USCSH then processes the internal form of the query, taking into account the level of proficiency of the user and the context of his current activities, and generates a response which is also in an internal form. This response is then passed on to the response-generation module of the natural-language interface, which generates an English response.

Authors: Sobczak, R.L.; Matthews, M.; Biswas, G.
Author full names: Sobczak, Ronald L. (6603851793); Matthews, Manton M. (7202525299); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890)
Title: A mechanism to automate the production of a keyword table for an intelligent active assistance system
Source title: Proceedings of SPIE - The International Society for Optical Engineering
Abstract: This paper describes the mechanism for generating a keyword table for an Intelligent Active Assistance System for small computers. This system runs under Unix (a trademark of AT&T Bell Laboratories). The mechanism outlined would be applicable for any system where hierarchically organized text in the form of screens is used to explain to the user a variety of concepts. The goal of the Assistance System is to help the user learn more about Unix and its capabilities. It accomplishes this in two ways. First, it allows the user to ask questions about different system features and commands. Second, it monitors the user, maintains a history, and prompts the user on more efficient command sequences. Because of these two modes, this system is considered both intelligent and active. The keyword table is an essential component of the Assistance System's ability to answer user questions. When a user enters a question on a particular UNIX feature or asks how to accomplish a specific task, the Assistance System recognizes the key concepts in the question and uses the keyword table to determine which screens contain the appropriate information for answering the question. Since UNIX has a large number of features, the Assistance System Knowledge Base has developed incrementally and the keyword table needs to be frequently updated to include references to the new information. Therefore, we have automated the production of the keyword table. Automation includes revising both the list of keywords and the references to screens containing each of the keywords. This is done in three steps: preparing a new list of keywords, pruning the list of unnecessary words, and searching the screens for references to each of the keywords. © 1987 SPIE. All rights reserved.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Bezdek, J.; Huang, L.-Y.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898); Huang, Liya (55492416000)
Title: Thesaurus building with transitive closures for kadre
Source title: Proceedings of SPIE - The International Society for Optical Engineering
Abstract: Currently there is a big thrust towards the development of automated, user-friendly online information retrieval systems, which are software packages that allow a user population to query and receive appropriate information that is stored in a computer database. Our research adopts a knowledge based approach to the design and development of KADRE (Knowledge Assisted Document Retrieval Expert), an experimental online document retrieval system. Knowledge based techniques allow us to incorporate some of an expert librarian’s heuristic techniques into the retrieval process. We present the mathematical model of the retrieval system as a five step process and discuss the system thesaurus in some detail. Six different transitive closures of the relational matrix of term pairs are used to compute the thesaurus, and the retrieval output produced by these techniques are compared. Representation of the thesaurus as a transitive closure offers two very significant advantages: this method provides a means for completion of partial knowledge which is represented as numerical relational data; and the ensuing completion has a well defined property of (mathematical) consistency. © 1987 SPIE.

Authors: Sobczak, R.L.; Matthews, M.; Biswas, G.
Author full names: Sobczak, Ronald L. (6603851793); Matthews, Manton M. (7202525299); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890)
Title: A response generation mechanism for an intelligent active assistance system
Source title: Proceedings of the 15th Annual Conference on Computer Science, CSC 1987
Abstract: This paper describes the response generator for an intelligent active assistance system for small computers. This system runs under Unix (a trademark of AT&T Bell Laboratories). A user can produce a response generator for any microcomputer using the techniques described here since the system is flexible, portable and can be tailored to the size and capabilities of the microcomputer. The goal of the assistance system is to help the user learn more about Unix and its capabilities. It accomplishes this in two ways. First it allows the user to ask questions about different system features and commands. Second it monitors the user, maintaining a history and prompting the user on more efficient command sequences. Because of these two modes, this system is considered both intelligent and active. © 1987 ACM.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Matthews, M.; Huang, A.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Matthews, Manton M. (7202525299); Huang, Antang (57201298614)
Title: An assistance system for unix text formatters
Source title: Proceedings of the 15th Annual Conference on Computer Science, CSC 1987
Abstract: This paper describes an active assistance system for the text formatters on the Unix system. The system is a subcomponent of USCSH, an active assistance interface for Unix being developed at the University of South Carolina. A complete user model has been designed to determine user proficiency in the use of text formatters. The model has two components: an overlay model and a process model. The overlay model is basically a collection of nodes that represent formatting concepts, commands, and tasks, structured in the form of a conceptual hierarchy. The process model infers what kind of text formatting tasks the user is involved in, and if he is making errors, or using inefficient methods in executing them. Using the above information, a prompting mechanism provides the user with messages that explain how certain tasks can be done in an efficient way, using commands that the user does not know about, or a sequence of commands that he has not previously used. Besides, the prompting mechanism also generates "periodic" prompts, to inform the user about formatting commands and tasks that he is unware off. © 1987 ACM.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Bezdek, J.; Subramanian, V.; Marques, M.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898); Subramanian, Viswanath (57209519235); Marques, Marisol M. (7201744057)
Title: Knowledge‐assisted document retrieval: II. The retrieval process
Source title: Journal of the American Society for Information Science
Abstract: This article presents our conceptual model of the retrieval process of a document‐retrieval system. The retrieval mechanism input is an unambiguous intermediate form of a user query generated by the language processor using the method described previously. Our retrieval mechanism uses a two‐step procedure. In the first step a list of documents pertinent to the query are obtained from the document database, and then an evidence‐combination scheme is used to compute the degree of support between the query and individual documents. The second step uses a ranking procedure to obtain a final degree of support for each document chosen, as a function of individual degrees of support associated with one or more parts of the query. The end result is a set of document citations presented to the user in ranked order in response to the information request. Numerical examples are given to illustrate various facets of the overall system, which has been prototypically implemented in modular form to test system response to changes in model parameters. © 1987 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Copyright © 1987 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Bezdek, J.; Marques, M.; Subramanian, V.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898); Marques, Marisol M. (7201744057); Subramanian, Viswanath (57209519235)
Title: Knowledge‐assisted document retrieval: I. The natural‐language interface
Source title: Journal of the American Society for Information Science
Abstract: In this article we describe the conceptual model and processing of (constrained) natural‐language queries in information retrieval systems. A language interface based on fuzzy set techniques is proposed to handle the uncertainty inherent in natural‐language semantics. The conceptual model is developed and exemplified in the context of document retrieval. Specifically, the user query is considered to be a triple, q = (q<inf>c</inf>, q<inf>y</inf>, q<inf>n</inf>), where q<inf>c</inf> indicates the part of the query that deals with concepts and operators that link these concepts, q<inf>y</inf> identifies the publication period the user is interested in, and q<inf>n</inf>, pertains to the number of documents to be retrieved. We describe query decomposition using an augmented transition network parser and the assignment of functions and relations needed by each portion of the query to represent uncertainties inherent in the natural language. The output of the natural‐language interface is then passed to a knowledge‐based retrieval mechanism that will be described in a companion article (Part II). © 1987 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Copyright © 1987 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

Authors: Rao Padala, A.M.; Biswas, G.; Bose, P.K.
Author full names: Rao Padala, Adithya M. (6507297319); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Bose, Prasanta K. (57203070809)
Title: ASSUMPTION BASED REASONING APPLIED TO PERSONAL FLIGHT PLANNING.
Abstract: The dynamic formulation and subsequent reformulation of constraints in an interactive problem-solving environment are discussed. Globe-Trotter, a personal flight plant scheduler, has been implemented on the Explorer, the Texas Instruments Lisp machine. This system, much like a human travel agent, goes through a series of interactions with travelers and helps them select flights that satisfy most of their constraints. Globe-Trotter uses assumption-based reasoning and truth maintenance techniques to drive the interaction. The system also uses RTMS, the relational table management system, to generate queries for the airline flight schedule database.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Abramczyk, R.; Oliff, M.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Abramczyk, Robert (6505862683); Oliff, Michael D. (6602085201)
Title: OASES: An Expert System for Operations Analysis-The System for Cause Analysis
Source title: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man and Cybernetics
Abstract: An application of knowledge-based systems in the field of business decisionmaking is presented. The expert's reasoning processes and problem-solving techniques in the operations analysis domain are the primary focus, and OASES, an operations analysis expert system, is designed to play the role of an intelligent assistant and to aid management in diagnosing problems in production processes, such as automobile assembly or textile manufacturing. The system combines forward and backward inferencing mechanisms to interact with users in a mixed initiative format. The knowledge base is designed as a partitioned rule base, and the inference engine uses the Dempster-Shafer scheme for inexact reasoning. Copyright © 1987 by The Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers, Inc.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Bezdek, J.; Oakman, R.L.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898); Oakman, Robert L. (16459935700)
Title: A knowledge-based approach to online document retrieval system design
Source title: Proceedings of the ACM SIGART International Symposium on Methodologies for Intelligent Systems, ISMIS 1986
Abstract: A knowledge-based system approach is applied to the design of a document retrieval system for online retrieval of bibliographic material. The main components of the system are a language interface that understands user queries in pseudo natural language (i.e. queries that are restricted to subject domain concepts), a retrieval component that combines the expertise of subject-domain and retrieval experts, a help system that AIDS users in formulating and reformulating queries, and a user model builder that infers specific user characteristics. A comprehensive mathematical model based on fuzzy sets and fuzzy relations has been defined for the thesaurus and the retrieval mechanism and a prototypical system has been implemented in a modular fashion to test system response to changes in model parameters. © 1986 Association for Computing Machinery Inc. All rights reserved.
Author Keywords: Knowledge based systems; Natural language interface; Online document retrieval; Retrieval functions
Open Access: All Open Access; Gold Open Access

Authors: Bezdek, J.; Biswas, G.; Huang, L.-Y.
Author full names: Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Huang, Liya (55492416000)
Title: Transitive closures of fuzzy thesauri for information-retrieval systems
Source title: International Journal of Man-Machine Studies
Abstract: In this paper we represent a thesaurus (R) for an information system as the sum of two fuzzy relations,S(synonyms) and G(generalizations). The max-star completion of R is defined as R¯*, the max-star transitive closure of R. We interpret R¯*, which extends the concept-pair fuzzy relation R initially provided by an expert, as a linguistic completion of the thesaurus. Six max-star completions, corresponding to six well-known T- norms, are defined, analysed, and numerically illustrated on a nine-term dictionary. The application of our results in the context of document retrieval is this: one may use R¯* as a means of effecting replacements of terms appearing in a natural-language document request. The weights (R¯*) ij can be used to diminish or increase one's confidence in the degree of support being developed for each document considered relevant to a given query. The ijth element of R¯* can be regarded as the ultimate extent to which term j can be “reached” from term i; the values in R¯* thus represent degrees of confidence in max-star transitive chains. © 1986, Academic Press Inc. (London) Limited. All rights reserved.

Authors: Matthews, M.; Biswas, G.
Author full names: Matthews, Manton M. (7202525299); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890)
Title: RAISING USER PROFICIENCY THROUGH ACTIVE ASSISTANCE: AN INTELLIGENT EDITOR.
Abstract: A description is given of an application of a knowledge-based systems approach to the design of active assistance interfaces. In contrast with almost all current assistance systems which respond only when information is requested, an active assistance system will on occasion take the initiative and offer a suggestion to the user. As a prototype of a general active assistance interface the authors have developed an intelligent editor, LEVI (LEarning VI) which actively guides the user in becoming more proficient in using the system. LEVI uses explicit models of the capabilities of the user and of the capabilities of the system. The decisions of when to prompt and on what to prompt next are made using a collection of heuristic rules.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Oliff, M.; Sen, Arun
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Oliff, Michael D. (6602085201); Sen, Arun (59886188300)
Title: DESIGN OF AN EXPERT SYSTEM IN OPERATIONS ANALYSIS.
Abstract: The authors describe the design of an expert system in operations analysis. The system plays the role of an intelligent assistant or a consultant and assists the management staff in a production environment with their trouble-shooting activities. The details of the knowledge base and the inference mechanism of a first prototype system are presented.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Matthews, C.T.; Matthews, M.; Rabon, P.; Wilhite, R.L.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Matthews, C. T. (7201785609); Matthews, Manton M. (7202525299); Rabon, P. (6507904994); Wilhite, R. L. (6507479911)
Title: ACTIVE ASSISTANCE SYSTEM FOR 'VI'.
Abstract: An active assistance system for 'vi,' a screen-based editor that runs under Unix, is discussed. The intelligent component of the assistance system answers user queries about the capabilities of the system and how to perform specific tasks. The active component acts much like an expert advisor by monitoring user interaction with the system and prompting the user when he is using the system inefficiently or making repeated errors. In addition, the system also periodically prompts a user about features not used before by the particular user. The aim is to build an assistance system that not only provide users easy access to vi capabilities, but also provide a mechanism that enables them to gradually learn and become experts in the use of vi.

Authors: Matthews, M.; Biswas, G.
Author full names: Matthews, Manton M. (7202525299); Biswas, Gautam (57211726890)
Title: ORACLE: A KNOWLEDGEABLE USER INTERFACE.
Source title: Proceedings - IEEE Computer Society's International Computer Software & Applications Conference
Abstract: The authors present the design of ORACLE, an active and intelligent assistance system that overcomes the shortcomings of a number of existing user interfaces. ORACLE operates in many ways like a human expert, constantly observing what the user is typing. On occasion the system observes things that the user could have done in a more effective manner and, taking into consideration the level of experience of the user, suggests how he/she might have accomplished the task more efficiently. ORACLE has two major components: an active assistant and an intelligent assistant. The intelligent component of the facility allows a user to make natural language queries about the use of the software system. Responses are tailored to the user's proficiency in the use of the software system and current session characteristics. The active component monitors user interactions with the system to 'learn' his proficiency and determine current session characteristics. Using this information, it makes decisions about when to prompt a user and suggest more efficient methods for accomplishing certain tasks.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Subramanian, V.; Marques, M.; Bezdek, J.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Subramanian, Viswanath (57209519235); Marques, Marisol M. (7201744057); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898)
Title: DOCUMENT RETRIEVAL SYSTEM USING A FUZZY EXPERT SYSTEMS APPROACH.
Abstract: The authors discuss a document retrieval system based on a fuzzy expert systems approach. The system is designed to retrieve documents in the area of knowledge representation in AI. User queries are interpreted and converted into an intermediate form for processing by the retrieval mechanism. The retrieval mechanism uses a two-step approach. In the first step, a pruned list of documents pertinent to the query is produced. In the second step, an evidence combination scheme based on fuzzy logic and the Dempster-Shafer theory is used to compute a relevance measure between the query and individual documents. A ranked list of documents is then presented to the user.

Authors: Biswas, G.; Subramanian, V.; Bezdek, J.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Subramanian, Viswanath (57209519235); Bezdek, Jim C. (7005397898)
Title: KNOWLEDGE BASED SYSTEM APPROACH TO DOCUMENT RETRIEVAL.
Abstract: A discussion is presented of the design and implementation of a prototype document retrieval system using a knowledge-based approach. Both the domain-specific knowledge base and the inferencing schemes are based on a fuzzy-set-theoretic framework. Concepts that describe domain topics and relationships between concepts are captured in a knowledge base. Documents are represented as concept-weight pairs and clustered into a few general subsets. The retrieval mechanism uses a two-step approach. In the first step, a pruned list of documents pertinent to the query is produced. In the second step, an evidence combination scheme is used to compute a degree of relevance between the query and individual documents retrieved in step 1. Finally, a set of document citations are presented to the user in ranked order as an answer to his query.

Authors: Sen, Arun; Biswas, G.
Title: Decision support systems: An expert systems approach
Source title: Decision Support Systems
Abstract: This paper provides a conceptual framework for designing decision support systems (DSS) using an expert systems approach. Currently there is a significant trend towards the use of knowledge-based systems techniques in DSS design, but a comprehensive framework is yet to be proposed. Our paper addresses this problem and presents such a framework. Efforts are currently underway to design, implement and test a system based on this framework. © 1985.
Author Keywords: Decision support; Decision support systems; Expert systems

Authors: Biswas, G.; Dubes, R.C.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Dubes, Richard C. (7004608101)
Title: Some experiments in two-dimensional grammatical inference
Source title: Pattern Recognition Letters
Abstract: This paper presents results of experiments conducted on a scheme for inferring two-dimensional, probabilistic Siromoney array grammars incorporating Markov random field distortion of binary images. © 1984.
Author Keywords: Array grammars; Markov random field model

Authors: Biswas, G.; Jain, A.K.; Dubes, R.C.
Author full names: Biswas, Gautam (57211726890); Jain, Anil K. (36071504600); Dubes, Richard C. (7004608101)
Title: Evaluation of Projection Algorithms
Source title: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
Abstract: A number of linear and nonlinear mapping algorithms for the projection of patterns from a high-dimensional space to two dimensions are available. These two-dimensional representations allow quick visual observation of a data set. A combination of two popular mapping algorithms-Sammon‘s mean-square error technique and the triangulation method-is proposed to overcome the limitations in the individual algorithms. Some factors which describe the goodness of a projection are described, and a comparison is made of six of these algorithms by running them on four data sets. The results obtained support the use of the proposed algorithm. Copyright © 1981 by The Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers, Inc.
Author Keywords: Exploratory data analysis; linear mapping; minimum spanning tree; nonlinear mapping; pattern recognition; two-dimensional projection
